text.title: Attribution Model Types and Platform Defaults
text.summary: Defines the six attribution models, explains the cross-platform overlap problem, and provides lookback window guidelines by sales cycle length.

section.title: Attribution Models

rule.statement: Select an attribution model based on sales cycle length and available conversion volume, not platform convenience.
rule.rationale: Each model assigns credit differently and will produce different CPA and ROAS numbers for the same spend. Using the wrong model leads to systematically over- or under-investing in channels.

section.p: Model comparison for a journey: FB click → Google click → Email click → Purchase

list.item: Last-click: FB 0% / Google 0% / Email 100% — conservative, ignores journey
First-click: FB 100% / Google 0% / Email 0% — awareness focus only
Linear: FB 33% / Google 33% / Email 33% — equal credit across all touches
Position-based: FB 40% / Google 20% / Email 40% — weights first and last touch
Time-decay: FB 10% / Google 30% / Email 60% — more credit to recent touches
Data-driven: ML-based — requires 50+ conversions/channel/month to train reliably

rule.statement: Document the attribution model and lookback window for every platform in use; never assume defaults are consistent across platforms.
rule.rationale: Each platform uses different defaults: Meta counts 7-day click + 1-day view, Google Ads uses last Google click at 30 days, GA4 uses data-driven at 90 days. Comparing numbers without knowing this is meaningless.

list.item: Google Ads: last Google click, 30-day window

section.title: The Overlap Problem
section.p: When a user clicks a Meta ad then a Google ad before purchasing, both platforms record the conversion. Platform totals routinely exceed actual sales by 20-40%.

antipattern.description: Comparing platform-reported conversions directly across channels to determine which channel is "winning."
antipattern.reason: Platform totals are not deduplicated. A user clicking both Meta and Google appears in both reports as a unique conversion. The comparison is meaningless without a unified view.

rule.statement: Use GA4 or a warehouse-deduped view as the single source of truth for cross-channel performance; use platform data only for in-platform optimization.
rule.rationale: GA4 deduplicates across channels using the same user session. Platform data remains useful for optimizing bids and creative within that platform's ecosystem.

rule.statement: Match lookback window to actual sales cycle; never use a shorter window to make numbers look better.
rule.rationale: A 7-day window on a 30-day SaaS trial cycle will miss the majority of attributed conversions and undercount channel contribution.
